narrative: As a Hispanic male applying to law school, you've shared your strong Year 1 grades placing you in the 6th decile at your school, along with your undergraduate GPA of 3.00 and LSAT score of 35, while coming from an upper-middle-class family and planning to attend full-time. The admissions model, which predicts whether students like you are likely to pass the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 64% chance that you would fail it. This high predicted risk of failure is why your application was rejected—the school uses this prediction to prioritize applicants who show stronger signals of bar exam success. Among all the factors considered, your Year 3 grades, undergraduate GPA, and Year 1 grades had the strongest influence on pushing the model toward this concerning prediction. Your Year 3 decile ranking of 9th—well above the typical student's 5.5th decile—actually worked strongly in your favor, pulling the prediction toward expecting you to pass the bar. In contrast, your undergraduate GPA of 3.00, slightly below the average of 3.21, was the biggest driver toward failure, as lower GPAs like yours often correlate with bar struggles in the model's view. Your solid Year 1 decile of 6th, a bit above the average of 5.7th, unfortunately nudged things slightly toward failure too, perhaps because it wasn't quite high enough to offset other concerns. Compared to typical students, your LSAT of 35 is close to the average 36.7 but didn't carry as much weight here, and your full-time status aligns with most applicants. While your background as a Hispanic male from an upper-middle-income family is part of the picture, it was these academic markers that tipped the scales most decisively. This doesn't define your potential—many students improve dramatically with targeted effort. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT or bolstering your GPA through post-baccalaureate courses, and seek programs with strong bar prep support tailored to diverse students like yourself. Reapplying after addressing these key areas could shift the model's view in your favor next time.
